去年10月，一场大火烧毁了加州斯托克顿的公寓楼，劳拉·基德-普卢默(Laura Kidd-Plummer)曾在那里生活了五年。大约十年前，今年将满70岁的基德-普卢默(Kidd-Plummer)从她在奥克兰体育馆(Oakland Coliseum)服装部的工作退休，她在那里工作了21年。为了寻找更便宜的房租，她最终搬到了斯托克顿。大火过后，她和她的狗Poopee无家可归，Poopee是波美拉尼亚和约克混血儿。从那以后，她告诉我，“我只是想让我的头浮出水面。”她在一家汽车旅馆住了几个月，这是有保险赔偿的，然后和熟人住在一起。今年5月，她搬进了北斯托克顿的一套阁楼。她能够用她作为斯托克顿经济赋权示范(Stockton Economic Empowerment Demonation)参与者获得的资金为这套公寓支付定金，这是一个基本收入试点项目，在过去一年半的时间里，该项目每月向个人提供500美元的无条件现金转移。她说，以前，基德-普卢默“有信用卡，不得不用它们吃饭”，因为她每月只能通过食品券领取16美元。现在她可以买食物了。“我一直都在工作，所以大多数项目我都没有资格参加，”她告诉我。“我没想到会被选中。当我收到邮寄的信时，我惊呆了。“。
这项由斯托克顿市市长迈克尔·塔布斯(Michael Tubbs)带头的计划原定于今年夏天结束：本月的付款将是最后一笔。5月下旬，塔布斯宣布将延长至2021年1月，以应对冠状病毒大流行给参与者带来的经济压力。虽然延长该计划的想法甚至在-19传播之前就已经在讨论中，但塔布斯告诉我，目前的条件使得这样做成为一种“道德上的必要性”，因为许多参与者已经失去了工作，那些被归类为基本工作者的人面临着更大的风险。-19让人们关注的事实是，许多美国人生活在经济混乱的持续时刻，因为经济的基本面一直不起作用，“他告诉我。
现年29岁的塔布斯是斯托克顿第一位黑人市长，也是有史以来最年轻的市长。在市议会任职4年后，他以专注于从2008年崩盘中复苏的政纲竞选市长，并于2016年以70%的选票当选，击败了一位饱受一系列丑闻困扰的现任市长。已经依赖了大量的外部资金，就像塔布斯追求的其他几个项目一样，包括一个由2000万美元的私人捐款运行的教育倡议。塔布斯第一次接触全民基本收入(UBI)的概念是在2009年，当时他还是斯坦福大学的一名本科生，在一门课程中，他讲述了小马丁·路德·金(Martin Luther King，Jr.)晚年对这一概念的倡导。2017年塔布斯上任后不久，作为他的工作人员关于解决贫困问题的研究项目的一部分，向一群斯托克顿居民发放无条件付款的可能性就出现了。斯托克顿有20%的居民生活在贫困线以下，贫困线远远高于州平均水平，有色人种居民受到的影响不成比例。尽管如此，塔布斯最初还是持怀疑态度-他担心资金问题，并认为这个想法可能会被证明不受选民欢迎。“这是我第一次当选，”他告诉我。“我不希望这是我最后一次参加比赛。”
然而，当塔布斯会见了经济安全项目(Economic Security Project)的联合主席时，这个计划开始成形。经济安全项目是一个由Facebook联合创始人克里斯·休斯(Chris Hughes)发起的基本收入倡导组织。该组织正在寻找一个测试飞行员的城市，并给了斯托克顿100万美元的赠款。(该项目的延期将通过私人捐赠单独提供资金。)。这笔资金允许125名参与者每人每月获得500美元，这一数额是基于数据显示，大约40%的美国人负担不起400美元的紧急费用。该项目的100名接受者是匿名的，而包括Kidd-Plummer在内的其余人则自愿公开谈论他们的经历。这项研究旨在证明，根据研究计划，基本收入可以“降低每月收入的波动性，并提供更大的收入充足，这将反过来减少心理压力，改善身体功能。”我们联系了随机抽样的社区居民，这些社区的人口处于或低于斯托克顿的收入中位数水平。在被选中的人中，约有43%的人报告说自己受雇于全职或兼职。其中10%是照顾者，这一群体往往没有资格获得失业和其他福利。塔布斯告诉我，他并不认为基本收入特别激进，相反，他认为基本收入是“这一代人安全网的延伸”，追随了社会保障、儿童等事业的发展道路
斯坦福大学(Stanford University)历史学教授詹妮弗·伯恩斯(Jennifer Burns)告诉我，两党对该法案的支持标志着人们对现金转移的想法发生了重大转变。尽管林登·约翰逊(Lyndon Johnson)和理查德·尼克松(Richard Nixon)都曾短暂考虑过各种形式的UBI-Johnson作为反贫困战争的一部分，尼克松也曾将其作为家庭援助计划的一个要素-但从历史上看，现金转移并不是一个广泛受欢迎的政治想法。尼克松的提案未能在参议院获得通过，右翼批评其“奖励浪费行为”，正如伯恩斯所说，左翼批评其危及现有项目。最终，这个想法被修改为劳动所得税抵免，这是一种针对低收入美国人的退税制度，与基本收入计划有一些相似之处，但以工作为条件。最近对UBI的呼吁大多来自硅谷，在硅谷，倾向于自由意志主义的企业家接受了这一概念，认为这是解决由于自动化程度提高而导致的失业的一种捷径。根据伯恩斯的说法，目前的危机已经将焦点从假想的灾难转移到了已经存在的不平等上。在她看来，自动化的争论主要是让人分心，但“如果对人工智能的担忧有助于人们环顾四周，思考已经在进行的事情，那就很好。”
的目标始终是在州或联邦一级促进基本收入计划的采用，而不是为斯托克顿的长期计划奠定基础。现在，随着该项目进入最后阶段，其创建者已经收到了来自其他城市正在开发的试点项目的建议请求，包括西雅图、波特兰、芝加哥、纽瓦克、纳什维尔和新奥尔良。上个月，美国市长会议通过了一项由塔布斯领导的决议，敦促“城市、州和联邦政府探索保证收入的可行性”，以回应“-19揭示了经济不安全并暴露了我们目前福利体系的脆弱性”的方式。缅因州参议员申娜·贝洛斯(Shenna Bellows)正在领导一个关于UBI的研究委员会，她告诉我，近几个月来，她的选民“真的在为拜占庭式的失业和小企业救济计划而苦苦挣扎”，她希望“这场危机能为更简单的解决方案指明方向”。
塔布斯认为，联邦现金转移计划的潜力主要取决于政治意愿。“这个国家有想办法支付我们认为重要的东西的历史，”他告诉我。加州大学伯克利分校(University of California，Berkeley)经济学家希拉里·霍恩斯(Hilary Hoynes)表示，在美国，一个真正普及的联邦基本收入计划每年的成本约为3万亿美元，约占目前联邦总支出的五分之三。她认为，一个更可行的模式可以，至少在开始时，像该法案那样的结构，收入下限设定得比目前的福利计划高得多。(大约80%的斯托克顿居民有资格参加。)。虽然这一模式缺乏普适性，但它将解决目前可用产品中的一个关键缺陷。正如霍恩斯所说：“美国提供的援助越来越多是有条件的，而且，正如现在非常明显的那样，以工作为条件的项目在工作不充足的地方是没有帮助的。”伯恩斯告诉我，即使关于UBI的讨论减少了，它的反对者“至少必须参与到这个想法中来，因为它满足了危机的许多需求。”
note_en:
Last October, a fire tore through the apartment complex in Stockton, California, where Laura Kidd-Plummer had lived for five years. Nearly a decade earlier, Kidd-Plummer, who will turn seventy this year, had retired from her job in the wardrobe department at the Oakland Coliseum, where she had worked for twenty-one years. She eventually moved to Stockton in search of cheaper rent. After the fire, she and her dog, Poopee, a Pomeranian-Yorkie mix, were left homeless. Ever since, she told me, “I’m just trying to keep my head above water.” She stayed in a motel for a couple of months, which was covered by insurance, and then with acquaintances. In May, she moved into a loft in North Stockton. She was able to make the deposit on the apartment using funds she has received as a participant in the Stockton Economic Empowerment Demonstration, or  , a basic-income pilot program that has provided unconditional cash transfers of five hundred dollars per month to individuals over the last year and a half. Before  , Kidd-Plummer “had credit cards and had to use them to eat,” she said, because she was only eligible for sixteen dollars per month through food stamps. Now she’s able to cover food. “I’ve always worked, so most programs I’m not eligible for,” she told me. “I didn’t expect to be chosen. When I got the letter in the mail, I was floored.”
  The program, spearheaded by Stockton’s mayor, Michael Tubbs, was scheduled to end this summer: this month’s payment was slated to be the last. In late May, Tubbs announced that   would be extended through January, 2021, in response to the economic strain put on participants by the  coronavirus pandemic. While the idea of extending the program had been under discussion even before the spread of  -19, Tubbs told me that current conditions made doing so a “moral imperative,” as many participants have lost work, and those classified as essential workers face increased risk. “ -19 has put the focus on the fact that a lot of Americans live in constant moments of economic disruption, because the fundamentals of the economy haven’t been working,” he told me.
 Tubbs, who is twenty-nine, is Stockton’s first Black mayor, and its youngest ever. After four years serving on the city council, he ran for mayor on a platform focussed on recovery from the 2008 crash, and was elected, in 2016, with seventy per cent of the vote, defeating an incumbent plagued by a string of scandals.   has relied on significant outside funding, as have several other projects that Tubbs has pursued, including an education initiative that has been run on a twenty-million-dollar private donation. Tubbs first encountered the concept of a universal basic income, or U.B.I., while he was an undergraduate at Stanford, in 2009, in a course that covered Martin Luther King, Jr.,’s  advocacy for the idea late in his life. The possibility of issuing unconditional payments to a group of Stockton residents came up soon after Tubbs took office, in 2017, as a part of his staff’s research project on addressing poverty.  Twenty per cent of Stockton’s residents fall below the poverty line, which is well above the state average, and residents of color are disproportionately affected. Still, Tubbs was initially skeptical—he worried about funding and thought that the idea could prove unpopular with voters. “This was my first time being elected,” he told me. “I didn’t want it to be my last.”
  The plan started to take shape, though, when Tubbs met the co-chair of the Economic Security Project, a basic-income advocacy group launched by the Facebook co-founder Chris Hughes. The organization was looking for a city in which to test a pilot, and gave Stockton a million-dollar grant. (The program’s extension will be funded separately, through a private donation.) The funding allowed a hundred and twenty-five participants each to receive five hundred dollars a month, an amount that was based on data indicating that around forty per cent of Americans can’t afford a four-hundred-dollar emergency expense. A hundred recipients in the program are anonymous, while the rest, including Kidd-Plummer, have volunteered to speak publicly about their experience. The study set out to prove that a basic income could, according to the research plan, “lead to reductions in monthly income volatility and provide greater income sufficiency, which will in turn lead to reduced psychological stress and improved physical functioning.” A random sample of residents in neighborhoods with populations that are at or below Stockton’s median income level were contacted. Around forty-three per cent of those who were chosen reported being employed either full or part time. Ten per cent of them are caregivers, a group that often fails to qualify for unemployment and other benefits. Tubbs told me that he doesn’t see a basic income as particularly radical but, instead, as “this generation’s extension of the safety net,” following in the path of things like Social Security, child-labor laws, weekends, and collective bargaining.
   Two professors of social work, Amy Castro Baker, of the University of Pennsylvania, and Stacia Martin-West, of the University of Tennessee, who have conducted research together on the gender and racial wealth gaps, were brought on to help design the pilot and evaluate the results. Stockton, they told me, was an ideal location: as of 2018, it was the  most diverse city in the country; many of its residents moved from more expensive parts of the Bay Area and continue to commute into those cities for low-paying jobs; and it is, historically, the  foreclosure capital of the United States. In designing the logistics of the program, they emphasized “rethinking our safety net with an anchor of justice and dignity,” as Castro Baker put it, while addressing “inequality predicated on racism and sexism.”
  Each month, participants receive payments on a debit card, which the researchers are able to track. No restrictions are placed on what the money can be used for; if participants don’t want their expenses tracked or prefer to use cash, they can transfer the money off the card. Castro Baker and Martin-West have also been studying the well-being of people in a control group who did not receive payments. Members of both groups complete monthly check-ins, during which they provide updates and summarize their mental state by using emojis. During the study, the researchers have released data on an online  dashboard, where observers can read stories from participants and see spending trends change over time. For example, during the pandemic, the percentage of money that participants spent on food, consistently the largest category, reached nearly twenty-five per cent over the monthly average, while the amount spent on recreation dropped to less than two per cent.
  Participants have also put the money toward rent, car payments, and paying off debt, as well as one-off expenses for themselves or their children: dental surgery, a prom dress, football camp, and shoes. They’ve also been able to cut back on working second and third jobs; one participant, a forty-eight-year-old mother of two who works full time at Tesla, was able to stop working as a delivery driver for DoorDash. Alcohol and tobacco has accounted for less than one per cent of spending per month.
  Since   was launched, the idea of a basic income, which has long been considered a fringe idea, has gained at least provisional support from  the Pope,  Jack Dorsey, and  Nancy Pelosi. Andrew Yang made U.B.I. a centerpiece of his Presidential campaign. More recently, the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security ( ) Act, which is  estimated to have kept twelve million people from sinking into poverty, included direct cash payments to every American with an annual income below seventy-five thousand dollars, and there are currently eight bills in Congress proposing some form of additional cash transfer to back up the checks that have already gone out. In May, eighty-four members of Congress signed a joint  letter asking that monthly direct payments be included in future relief bills as “the most efficient mechanism for delivering economic relief to those most at-risk in this crisis.”
 Jennifer Burns, a history professor at Stanford University, told me that the bipartisan support for the   Act marked a significant shift in thinking about cash transfers. Although both Lyndon Johnson and Richard Nixon briefly considered forms of U.B.I.—Johnson as part of the War on Poverty, and Nixon as an element of the Family Assistance Plan—cash transfers have not, historically, been a widely popular political idea. Nixon’s proposal, which failed to pass in the Senate, was criticized on the right for “rewarding wasteful behavior,” as Burns put it, and on the left for endangering existing programs. Ultimately, the idea was adapted to become the Earned Income Tax Credit, a system of rebates for low-income Americans that has some similarities to a basic-income program but is conditioned on work. Recent calls for U.B.I. have mostly come from Silicon Valley, where libertarian-leaning entrepreneurs embraced the concept as a quick fix for job losses due to increased automation. According to Burns, the current crisis has shifted the focus away from hypothetical disasters toward inequities that already exist. In her view, the automation argument is primarily a distraction, but “if worrying about A.I. helps people look around and think about what’s already under way, that’s good.”
 The goal of   was always to promote the adoption of basic-income programs on a state or federal level, rather than to lay the groundwork for a long-term program in Stockton. Now, as the program moves into its final stretch, its creators have been flooded with requests for advice from pilot programs in development in other cities, including Seattle, Portland, Chicago, Newark, Nashville, and New Orleans. Last month, the U.S. Conference of Mayors adopted a resolution, led by Tubbs, that urges “cities, states, and the federal government to explore the feasibility of a guaranteed income” in response to the way in which “ -19 has shed light on economic insecurity and exposed the vulnerability of our current welfare system.” Shenna Bellows, a state senator in Maine who is leading a study committee on U.B.I., told me that, in recent months, her constituents “have really struggled with the byzantine unemployment and small-business-relief programs,” and she hopes “this crisis will point the way to simpler solutions.”
 Tubbs believes that the potential for a federal cash-transfer program hinges, primarily, on political will. “This country has a history of finding ways to pay for things that we say matter,” he told me. According to Hilary Hoynes, an economist at the University of California, Berkeley, the annual cost of a truly universal federal-basic-income program in the U.S. would be around three trillion dollars, which is approximately three-fifths of current total federal expenditures. She believes that a more viable model could, at least initially, be structured like the   Act and  , with an income cutoff set considerably higher than those of current welfare programs. (Around eighty per cent of Stockton residents were eligible to take part in  .) Though this model falls short of universality, it would address a key failing in what’s currently available. As Hoynes put it, “more and more of the assistance that’s provided in America is conditional, and, as is very evident right now, programs that are conditioned on work aren’t helpful where work isn’t plentiful.” Burns told me that, even if discussion of U.B.I. drops off, its opponents “have had to at least engage with the idea, because it met so many of the needs of the crisis.”
title: 全市基本收入试验的可喜结果
title_en: The Promising Results of a Citywide Basic Income Experiment

note:
在豪尔赫·路易斯·博尔赫斯(Jorge Luis Borges)短短12页的故事中，这位著名作家在巨大的记忆迷宫和拥有无限记忆能力的后果的背景下玩弄着无限。
1942年6月7日，在第二次世界大战改变了惯例的情况下，这是一个和往常一样的星期天。“国家报”(La Nación)的头版报道了英国的进攻，并继续轰炸鲁尔的德国工业区。在同一页上，人们可以读到日本舰队在中途岛造成的伤亡，以及英国步兵坦克袭击沙漠中的德军阵地。报纸的第五和第六页，在Eno的“水果盐”(一种助消化产品，每瓶售价70美分)和Fernet Branca(一种带朋友回家应该带回家的饮料)的广告间隙，讲述了门多萨一场没有受害者的地震，并宣布轮胎工厂可以开始恢复旧轮胎。在体育方面，阿根廷青年队在晋级英超联赛的征程中以4比1击败了阿尔西纳竞技队(Sportivo Alsina)，娱乐版面以每座1.5美元的价格宣传了一部由奥利维亚·德·哈维兰(Olivia De Havilland)和亨利·方达(Henry Fonda)主演的新电影。
1942年6月7日，根据La Nación的说法，这一天和其他任何一天都一样，只是艺术和文学版块上出现了一篇短篇小说，这篇短篇小说将把这一期报纸变成了一份历史性的文件。本周日副刊的第一页是斯特凡·茨威格(Stefan Zweig)的故事；第二页是埃内斯托·萨巴托(Ernesto Sabato)的一篇赞扬伽利略的文章；第三页几乎隐藏在人们的视线中，首次出现了豪尔赫·路易斯·博尔赫斯(Jorge Luis Borges)的不朽短篇小说“纪念的富内斯”(Funes The Memous)，配上亚历杭德罗·西里奥(Alejandro Sirio)的插图。
“纪念富内斯”讲述了来自弗雷·本托斯(Fray Bentos)的农民艾雷诺·富内斯(Ireneo Funes)的沧桑，他从马上摔下来，头部猛烈撞击，用令人难以置信的技能-或者可能是诅咒-完全记住了一切，恢复了意识。
Nosotros，de un vistazo，percibimos tres Copas en una Mesa；Funes，todos los vástagos y racimos y Frutos que comprende una Parra。SABía las formas de las nubes australes del Amanecer del 30 de abril de 1882 y Podía comparlas en el Recuerdo con las Vetas de un library o en意大利面española que sólo había Mirado una vez y con las líneas de la esuma que un Remo Levantóen el Río Nero la víSpera de la ACCIón del Quebracho。
我们一下子就看到桌子上放着三个杯子；富内斯会看到一棵葡萄树上所有的嫩芽、花簇和果实。他知道1882年4月30日黎明时分南方云层的形状，并能在记忆中将它们与一本西班牙封面上只见过一次的条纹相提并论，也能与奎布拉乔战役前夕里奥黑人的桨在泡沫上激起的漩涡相提并论。]。
豪尔赫·路易斯·博尔赫斯(Jorge Luis Borges，1899-1986)在他的著作中深入探讨哲学和科学的重要问题，受到了普遍的好评。在博尔赫斯的手中，无限的主题要么是一个包含宇宙的点(“阿列夫”)，一个坚不可摧的迷宫(“两个国王和两个迷宫”)，一个永远重复的图书馆(“巴别塔图书馆”)，一个细分为无数可能性的故事(“岔路花园”)，或者是一幅非常详细的帝国地图，以至于它最终拥有帝国本身的大小(“严谨的科学”)。在“纪念的富内斯”(Funes The Memosous)中，博尔赫斯的故事只有12页，最终作为“菲西奥内斯”(Ficciones，1944)的一部分出版。博尔赫斯再次在一个同样引人入胜的背景下玩弄无限：巨大的记忆迷宫，以及拥有无限记忆能力的后果。
富内斯第一次被提到是在詹姆斯·乔伊斯的讣告中，1941年发表在“南方”杂志上的“关于乔伊斯的片段”。在那里，博尔赫斯带着一定程度的讽刺说，要直读像乔伊斯的“尤利西斯”这样的“怪物”-一部40万字的都柏林一天的重现-需要另一个能够记住无数细节的怪物。讣告的奇怪之处在于，博尔赫斯几乎没有提到乔伊斯或他的作品，而是描述了他当时正在撰写的故事的主人公爱雷诺·富内斯(Ireneo Funes)。
ENTERRE losobras que no he escito ni escribiré(pero que de alguna manera me retifican，siquiera miseriosa y rudimental)hay un relato de unas Ocho Diez páGinas cuyo funuso Diez páginas cuyo funuso se titiula“Funes el Memorioso”(“Funes el Memorioso”)：“Funes el Memorioso”。。。。德尔Comadrito mágico de mi cuento Cabe afirmar que es un前体de los Superhombres，un Zaratustra subburo y parcio；lo不可分割es que es un monstruco。Lo他记录了一个连续的讲演las cutrecientas mil palabras de ulise exigiría monstrucos análogos。
[在我没有写过也永远不会写的作品中(但这不知何故证明了我是合理的，无论我是多么神秘和简陋)，有一个短篇小说，大约有八到十页长，它丰富的草稿叫做“纪念的富内斯”。。。关于我故事中的魔术比喻，我可以说，他是超人的前身，是郊区残缺不全的查拉图斯特拉；不可否认的是，他是一个怪物。我之所以记住他，是因为要直截了当、不间断地阅读尤利西斯的四十万字，也需要类似的怪兽。]。
博尔赫斯在“菲西奥内斯”第二部分“诡计”的序言中指出，“纪念富内斯”是对失眠的长篇比喻。事实上，在故事接近尾声时，他提到富内斯发现很难入睡，因为睡觉就是从世界上分心。博尔赫斯在美国发表的一篇采访中详细介绍了他在自己的不眠之夜(可能是在阿德罗圭昆塔酒店一个潮湿的夏夜)构思Funes的方式：
当我失眠的时候，我试着忘记我自己，忘记我的身体，忘记我身体的位置，忘记床，忘记家具，忘记酒店的三个花园，忘记桉树，忘记书架上的书，忘记村子里所有的街道，忘记车站，忘记农舍。因为我忘不了，所以我一直保持清醒，睡不着觉。然后我对自己说，让我们假设有一个人无法忘记他所看到的一切，众所周知，这发生在詹姆斯·乔伊斯身上，他可能在一天内带来了“尤利西斯”，在这一天里发生了成千上万的事情。我想到了一个无法忘记那些事件的人，他最终被自己无限的记忆冲走了。一言以蔽之，那个支离破碎的流氓就是我，或者说是我为文学目的而盗取的形象，但这与我自己的失眠相对应。
在第一个千年的文献中已经提到了具有惊人记忆力的人，特别是在老普林尼的“自然历史”(自然历史)中，这是一种百科全书，在37本书中描述了从地理、科学和技术到古罗马的农业、草药和昆虫的一切。在第七卷第24章关于记忆的主题中，普林尼提到波斯国王居鲁士，他知道他所有士兵的名字；西皮略，他知道罗马所有人的名字；皮罗斯国王的大使西尼亚斯，他在抵达罗马后仅一天就知道了所有罗马参议员的名字；米特里达斯·尤帕托，他用他的帝国所说的22种语言主持司法；西蒙尼德斯，记忆法的发明者；或者希腊人查玛达斯，他会背诵。
普林尼认为拥有非凡的记忆力是一种福气。事实上，他在第七卷第24章一开始就说：“记忆是生活中最需要的恩惠，很多人都因此而声名远扬，所以很难说出谁在这方面做得最好，这是生活中最需要的恩惠。”(“记忆是人生最需要的恩赐，很难说谁在这方面做得最好，有那么多人因此而声名鹊起)”，他这样写着：“记忆是人生最需要的恩惠，很难说谁在这方面做得最好，有那么多人因此而声名鹊起。”
普林尼还描述了记忆的脆弱性，他认为，记忆可能会全部或部分由于疾病、伤害甚至恐慌而丢失。作为一个例子，他讲述了一个人在被石头击中后失去了命名字母的能力，以及另一个人在从屋顶上摔下来后忘记了某些人的故事。他还提到了演说家梅萨拉·科维纳斯(Messala Corvinus)，他甚至连自己的名字都记不起来了。
众所周知，博尔赫斯着迷于百科全书和“自然历史”(也许是历史上第一部百科全书)，事实上，他在“追忆的富内斯”中提到了这一点：富内斯向叙述者(博尔赫斯)索要任何拉丁文文本，博尔赫斯要求普林尼百科全书的第七卷和奎切拉的“同义词库”，就像这样，一旦发现一个人不能，卢布就会大失所望。然而，在他们的下一次会议上，富内斯用完美的拉丁语嘲弄地朗诵了一句话来欢迎博尔赫斯：“ut nihil non iisdem Verbis redderetur auditum”(字面意思是：“听过的东西都不能用同样的话重复”)。
通过富内斯，博尔赫斯和普林尼一样进入了记忆领域，尽管他的反应在一个关键方面与罗马人不同：普林尼认为拥有惊人的记忆能力是一种美德，而博尔赫斯的眼光则超越了这一点，并认为非凡的记忆力可能会成为一种诅咒。富内斯在故事进行到一半时说：
Más recuerdos tengo yo solo que Los que Habrán tenido todos los hombres desque el Mundo es mundo.。。。。我的记忆，先生，这是科莫和巴苏拉的Vaciadero de basura。
仅我一人的记忆就比世界存在以来所有人的记忆都要多。。。。先生，我的记忆就像垃圾堆。]。
鉴于其历史意义，普林尼的故事具有不可否认的价值。然而，要判断他们的真实性是不可能的，事实上，“自然历史”中描述的人物似乎更像是传奇人物，而不是真实人物(或许更能激起博尔赫斯的好奇心)。这在很大程度上是因为普林尼的许多描述都是基于口口相传的信息，在讲述过程中不可避免地发生了变化。例如，当普利尼在第七卷第21章描述惊人视力的情况时，他写道，荷马的“伊利亚特”是用如此小的剧本写成的，整个手稿可以用坚果壳来概括；他还提到了一个叫斯特拉博的人，他能认出135英里外的物体，在布匿战争期间，他能看到甚至数清停靠在西西里海角上迦太基的敌舰。
第一个记录在案的非凡记忆力案例是所罗门·谢列舍夫斯基(Solomon Shereshevskii)，他从20世纪20年代开始由著名的俄罗斯心理学家亚历山大·卢里亚(Alexander Luria)进行研究。正如Luria在他的书“记忆家的思想：一本关于巨大记忆的小书”(The Mind of a Mememonist：A Little Book About a Mememonist)中所写的那样，主题S(他提到舍列舍夫斯基是为了保护自己的名字)与其他所有人不同，如果他想忘记什么，就必须付出努力。谢列舍夫斯基拥有非常强的通感--不同感官之间的一种不由自主的联系，比如把数字和颜色联系起来--这给他的记忆赋予了更丰富的内容，从而使他的记忆更容易被回忆起来。这些联想，以及简单助记法的使用，让谢列舍夫斯基在第一次听到数字和字母多年后，就能记住它们的长序列。在研究舍列舍夫斯基30多年后，Luria承认他无法找到S.记忆的极限，考虑到它不是来自于一个业余爱好者，而是来自他那个时代最重要的心理学家之一，这是一个令人惊讶的说法。
谢列舍夫斯基和富内斯之间有明显的相似之处，尽管前者是基于他的通感训练他的记忆，而后者记忆一切都是完全自然的。然而，博尔赫斯不太可能知道卢里亚的作品，因为卢里亚在1968年才出版了他的书(英文版)，比博尔赫斯写下富内斯的故事晚了25年多。
“纪念富内斯”也展示了尼采的影响力(正如罗克珊娜·克里梅尔在一篇有趣的文章中所描述的那样)；特别是，博尔赫斯称富内斯是“超人的前身，一个郊区的、不完整的查拉图斯特拉(Zarathustra)”。在一篇关于遗忘重要性的精彩文章中，尼采写道：
试想一个最极端的例子，一个没有健忘能力的人，他该死地看到自己无处不在；这样的人将不再相信自己的存在，不再相信自己，将看到一切在动荡的粒子中四分五裂，迷失在这股形成的洪流中；就像赫拉克利特的真正学生一样，最终他几乎不敢动一根手指。所有的行动都需要遗忘，正如所有有机体的存在不仅需要光明，也需要黑暗。
博尔赫斯对心灵的迷恋(在这种哲学背景下，我再次使用“心灵”而不是“大脑”，尽管我没有对两者进行区分)可能来自他的父亲，一位律师和心理学教授，他把他介绍给了威廉·詹姆斯(William James)等作家，许多人认为威廉·詹姆斯是现代心理学之父。在他最重要的著作之一“心理学原理”(1890年)中，詹姆斯这样描述记忆：
如果我们什么都记得，在大多数情况下，我们应该像什么都不记得一样生病。。。。“矛盾的结果是，记住的一个条件是我们应该忘记。如果不是完全忘记了大量的意识状态，不是瞬间忘记了大量的状态，我们就完全记不起来了。“。
与富内斯、谢列舍夫斯基和尼采的关系令人着迷。例如，Luria写道，Shereshevskii“在逻辑组织方面相当无能”。博尔赫斯反过来说，富内斯。
您的位置：我也知道>地区>法国>瑞士法郎>葡萄牙语>索斯佩克，罪过禁运，没有时代可言。
毫不费力地学会了英语、法语、葡萄牙语、拉丁语。然而，我怀疑他的思考能力不是很强。]。
再说一遍：我不是指乔伊斯、普林尼、卢里亚、尼采和詹姆斯，而是要质疑博尔赫斯故事的原创性。相反，这些平行著作提供了哲学和科学基础，博尔赫斯可能在此基础上找到了他的部分灵感。撇开博尔赫斯是否知道卢里亚的研究--我相信不知道--的问题不谈，我不禁注意到，他在短篇小说的背景下对待一个像记忆这样复杂的话题时表现出的不可思议的清晰度。
回到富内斯和其他具有非凡记忆力的人身上，我们必须提到博尔赫斯本人，他可以用西班牙语、英语、德语和盎格鲁-撒克逊语以及其他语言引用整段文字。尽管失明可能导致了他令人难以置信的记忆(没有被视觉刺激分心，他可以像他之前的德莫克里特一样，专注于他的思想和记忆的溪流)，但博尔赫斯年轻时意识到，他会像他的父亲一样失去视力，这让他在还能看到的时候，对知识进行了不朽的追求。玛丽亚·科达玛(他的遗孀)记得，在她与博尔赫斯的第一次相遇中，他让她找一本书的节选。这位作家说，这段片段在书中间附近的奇数页上。小玉开始随机阅读一页，令人惊讶的是，博尔赫斯引导她到了正确的一页，尽管他已经失明多年，正如他在第一页上草草写下的那样，在1916年，也就是与小玉相遇的几十年前，他读过这本书。
罗德里戈·奎恩·奎罗加(Rodrigo Quian Quiroga)是莱斯特大学系统神经科学中心主任。他是“博尔赫斯与记忆”、“神经编码原理”、“脑电图成像大脑功能”和“健忘机”的作者。
note_en:
In Jorge Luis Borges's story of barely 12 pages, the celebrated writer plays with the infinite in a context of vast labyrinths of memory and the consequences of having an unlimited capacity to remember.
   June 7, 1942, was a Sunday like any other amid the altered routine of the Second World War. The front page of the newspaper La Nación reported on the British onslaught, which continued with a bombing campaign over the German industrial area in the Ruhr. On the same page, one could read about the casualties inflicted on the Japanese fleet at Midway and about British infantry tanks’ attacking German positions in the desert. Pages five and six of the paper, in between advertisements for Eno’s “Fruit Salt” (a digestive aid selling at 70 cents per vial) and Fernet Branca (a beverage that should be brought home as one brings a friend), give an account of an earthquake without victims in Mendoza and announce that tire factories can start restoring used tires. In sports, Argentinos Juniors beat Sportivo Alsina by four goals to one in their campaign to reach the premier league, and the entertainment pages promote Pirates of the Caribbean, in Technicolor, and a new movie starring Olivia de Havilland and Henry Fonda at $1.50 a seat.
  June 7, 1942, a day like any other according to La Nación, except for a short story appearing in the Arts and Letters section that would turn this issue of the newspaper into a historic document. The first page of this Sunday supplement features a story by Stefan Zweig; the second page contains an essay by Ernesto Sabato praising Galileo; and on the third page, almost hidden in plain sight, for the first time appears “Funes the Memorious,” Jorge Luis Borges’s monumental short story, with an illustration by Alejandro Sirio.
 “Funes the Memorious” tells the vicissitudes of Ireneo Funes, a peasant from Fray Bentos, who after falling off a horse and hitting his head hard recovers consciousness with the incredible skill — or perhaps curse — of remembering absolutely everything.
  Nosotros, de un vistazo, percibimos tres copas en una mesa; Funes, todos los vástagos y racimos y frutos que comprende una parra. Sabía las formas de las nubes australes del amanecer del 30 de abril de 1882 y podía compararlas en el recuerdo con las vetas de un libro en pasta española que sólo había mirado una vez y con las líneas de la espuma que un remo levantó en el Río Negro la víspera de la acción del Quebracho.
 [We, at a stroke, perceive three cups lying on a table; Funes would see all the shoots and clusters and fruit comprised by a vine. He knew the shapes of the southern clouds at dawn on April 30, 1882, and could compare them in his memory with the streaks on a book of Spanish cover that he had seen only once and with the swirls on the foam raised by an oar in the Río Negro on the eve of the battle of the Quebracho.]
  Jorge Luis Borges (1899–1986) has received universal acclaim for the depth with which he approached matters of philosophic and scientific import in his writings. In Borges’s hands, the topic of infinity comes alive either as a point that contains the universe (“The Aleph”), impregnable labyrinths (“The Two Kings and the Two Labyrinths”), a library that is eternally repeated (“The Library of Babel”), stories that subdivide into innumerable possibilities (“The Garden of Forking Paths”), or an imperial map so perfectly detailed that it ends up having the size of the empire itself (“Of Rigor in Science”). In “Funes the Memorious,” a story of barely 12 pages that was eventually published as part of “Ficciones” (1944), Borges again plays with the infinite in a context no less fascinating: the vast labyrinths of memory and the consequences of having an unlimited capacity to remember.
 Funes is first mentioned in an obituary of James Joyce, “A Fragment on Joyce,” published in 1941 in the magazine Sur. There, with some measure of sarcasm, Borges says that to read straight through a “monster” like Joyce’s “Ulysses” — a 400,000-word reconstruction of a single day in Dublin — requires another monster able to remember an infinite number of details. The strange thing about the obituary is that Borges barely refers to Joyce or his work and instead describes Ireneo Funes, the main character of the story he was writing at the time.
  Entre las obras que no he escrito ni escribiré (pero que de alguna manera me justifican, siquiera misteriosa y rudimental) hay un relato de unas ocho o diez páginas cuyo profuso borrador se titula “Funes el memorioso”. . . . Del compadrito mágico de mi cuento cabe afirmar que es un precursor de los superhombres, un Zaratustra suburbano y parcial; lo indiscutible es que es un monstruo. Lo he recordado porque la consecutiva y recta lectura de las cuatrocientas mil palabras de  Ulises exigiría monstruos análogos.
 [Among the works that I have not written and will never write (but that somehow justify me, in however mysterious and rudimentary a way) there is a short story, some eight to ten pages long, whose copious draft is entitled “Funes the Memorious.” . . . Of the magical  compadrito of my story I can state that he is a precursor to supermen, a suburban, incomplete Zarathustra; what cannot be denied is that he is a monster. I have remembered him because a straight, uninterrupted reading of  Ulysses’s four hundred thousand words would require similar monsters.]
 In the preface to “Artifices,” the second part of “Ficciones,” Borges argues that “Funes the Memorious” is a long metaphor of insomnia. In fact, toward the end of the story he mentions that Funes found sleeping difficult, because to sleep is to get distracted from the world. Borges gives more details on the way he conceived Funes during his own sleepless nights (perhaps during a sticky summer night at the  quinta in Adrogué), in an interview published in the United States:
 When I suffered from insomnia I tried to forget myself, to forget my body, the position of my body, the bed, the furniture, the three gardens of the hotel, the eucalyptus tree, the books on the shelf, all the streets of the village, the station, the farmhouses. And since I couldn’t forget, I kept on being conscious and couldn’t fall asleep. Then I said to myself, let us suppose there was a person who couldn’t forget anything he had perceived, and it’s well known that this happened to James Joyce, who in the course of a single day could have brought out “Ulysses,” a day in which thousands of things happened. I thought of someone who couldn’t forget those events and who in the end dies swept away by his infinite memory. In a word that fragmentary hoodlum is me, or is an image I stole for literary purposes but which corresponds to my own insomnia.
 Already in the literature of the first millennium there are references to people with prodigious memory, particularly in the “ Naturalis historia” (Natural History) of Pliny the Elder, a sort of encyclopedia that in 37 books describes everything from the geography, science, and technology to the agriculture, medicinal herbs, and insects of ancient Rome. In chapter 24 of book VII, on the topic of memory, Pliny mentions king Cyrus of Persia, who knew the names of all his soldiers; Scipio, who knew the names of all in Rome; Cineas, king Pyrrhus’s ambassador, who learned the names of all the Roman senators just one day after arriving in Rome; Mithridates Eupator, who administered justice in the 22 languages spoken in his empire; Simonides, inventor of mnemonics; or Charmadas the Greek, who could recite by heart any book from a library as though he were reading it.
 Pliny considers it a blessing to possess an extraordinary memory. In fact, he starts chapter 24 of book VII saying: “Memoria necessarium maxime vitae bonum cui praecipua fuerit, haut facile dictu est, tam multis eius gloriam adeptis [As to memory, the boon most necessary for life, it is not easy to say who most excelled in it, so many men having gained renown for it].”
  Pliny also describes the fragility of memory, arguing that it can be lost, in whole or in part, due to illness, injury, and even panic. As an example he tells the story of a man who lost the capacity to name letters after being struck by a stone, and of another who forgot certain people after falling from a roof. He also mentions Messala Corvinus, the orator, who lost recollection of even his own name.
 Borges, it is known, was fascinated by encyclopedias and by the “Naturalis historia” (perhaps the first encyclopedia in history), which in fact he mentions in “Funes the Memorious”: Funes asks the narrator (Borges) for any Latin text, and Borges obliges with volume VII of Pliny’s encyclopedia and Quicherat’s “Thesaurus,” just so the rube will be rudely disappointed upon finding out that one cannot learn such a complicated language using only a book and a dictionary. On their next meeting, however, Funes welcomes Borges by reciting, mockingly, in perfect Latin: “ut nihil non iisdem verbis redderetur auditum” (which, literally translated, means: “Nothing that has been heard can be repeated with the same words”).
 Through Funes, Borges, just like Pliny, enters the realm of memory, though his reaction differs from the Roman’s in a crucial regard: while Pliny considers it a virtue to have a prodigious capacity to remember, Borges looks beyond and argues that an extraordinary memory can become a curse. Says Funes, midway through the story:
 Más recuerdos tengo yo solo que los que habrán tenido todos los hombres desde que el mundo es mundo. . . . Mi memoria, señor, es como un vaciadero de basura.
 [I alone have more memories than all men may have ever had since the world exists. . . . My memory, sir, is like a rubbish heap.]
 Given their historical significance, Pliny’s stories are of undeniable value. It is, nonetheless, impossible to judge their veracity, and in fact the characters described in the “Naturalis historia” seem more legendary than real (perhaps arousing Borges’s curiosity even more). To a large extent this is due to the fact that many of Pliny’s descriptions are based on word-of-mouth information, inevitably altered in the telling. For example, when he describes cases of astonishing eyesight in chapter 21 of book VII, Pliny writes that Homer’s “Iliad” was written in such small script that the complete manuscript could fit in a nutshell; he also mentions a man called Strabo, who could recognize objects 135 miles away and who, during the Punic Wars, could sight and even count the enemy ships docked in Carthage from a promontory in Sicily.
  The first properly documented case of extraordinary memory is that of Solomon Shereshevskii, studied by the celebrated Russian psychologist Alexander Luria starting in the 1920s. As Luria reports in his book “ The Mind of a Mnemonist: A Little Book about a Vast Memory,” subject S. (as he refers to Shereshevskii to protect his name), unlike everyone else, had to make an effort if he wished to forget something. Shereshevskii possessed a very strong synesthesia — an involuntary link between different senses, like associating numbers with colors — that gave his memories a much richer content and thus made them easier to recollect. These associations, as well as the use of simple mnemonics, allowed Shereshevskii to remember long sequences of numbers and letters many years after first hearing them. After studying Shereshevskii for more than 30 years, Luria confessed his inability to find a limit to S.’s memory, a surprising statement considering that it comes not from an amateur but from one of the foremost psychologists of his time.
  There are clear parallels between Shereshevskii and Funes, despite the fact that the former trained his memory based on his synesthesia while for the latter to remember everything was completely natural. It is, however, unlikely that Borges knew of Luria’s work, since Luria published his book (in English) only in 1968, more than 25 years after Borges wrote the story of Funes.
 “Funes the Memorious” shows Nietzsche’s influence as well (as Roxana Kreimer describes in an interesting essay); in particular, Borges calls Funes “a precursor to supermen, a suburban, incomplete Zarathustra.” In a brilliant piece on the importance of forgetting, Nietzsche writes:
 Imagine the most extreme example, a human being who does not possess the power to forget, who is damned to see becoming everywhere; such a human being would no longer believe in his own being, would no longer believe in himself, would see everything flow apart in turbulent particles, and would lose himself in this stream of becoming; like the true student of Heraclitus, in the end he would hardly even dare to lift a finger. All action requires forgetting, just as the existence of all organic things requires not only light, but darkness as well.
 Borges’s fascination with the mind (in this philosophical context I again use “mind” rather than “brain,” though I make no distinction between the two) probably came from his father, a lawyer and psychology professor who introduced him to authors such as William James, considered by many to be the father of modern psychology. In “The Principles of Psychology” (1890), one of his foremost works, James says this about memory:
 If we remembered everything, we should on most occasions be as ill off as if we remembered nothing. . . . “The paradoxical result [is] that one condition of remembering is that we should forget. Without totally forgetting a prodigious number of states of consciousness, and momentarily forgetting a large number, we could not remember at all.”
 The relation to Funes, Shereshevskii, and Nietzsche is fascinating. Luria, for example, writes that Shereshevskii “was quite inept at logical organization.” Borges, in turn, says that Funes
 había aprendido sin esfuerzo el inglés, el francés, el portugués, el latín. Sospecho, sin embargo, que no era muy capaz de pensar.
 [had effortlessly learned English, French, Portuguese, Latin. I suspect, however, that he was not very capable of thinking.]
 Again: I do not refer to Joyce, Pliny, Luria, Nietzsche, and James so as to question the originality of Borges’s story. On the contrary, these parallel writings provide a philosophical and scientific foundation on which Borges may have found part of his inspiration. Leaving aside the issue of whether Borges knew of Luria’s studies or not — I believe not — I cannot help noticing the uncanny lucidity with which he treats a topic as complex as memory in the context of a short story.
 Going back to Funes and other people with extraordinary memory, we must mention Borges himself, who could quote whole passages in Spanish, English, German, and Anglo-Saxon, among other tongues. Though it is possible that blindness may have contributed to his incredible memory (not being distracted by visual stimuli, he could focus, like Democritus before him, on his thoughts and the stream of his remembrance), Borges’s youthful realization that he, like his father, would lose his eyesight took him on a monumental quest for knowledge while he could still see. María Kodama [his widow] remembers that, on one of her first encounters with Borges, he asked her to find an excerpt from a book. The fragment, the writer said, was on an odd-numbered page near the middle of the book. Kodama started to read a page at random and Borges, amazingly, guided her to the right page even though he had been blind for many years and — as he jotted on the first page — had read the book in 1916, decades before this encounter with Kodama.
  Rodrigo Quian Quiroga is Director of the Centre for Systems Neuroscience at the University of Leicester. He is the author of “ Borges and Memory,” “ Principles of Neural Coding,” “ Imaging Brain Function with EEG,” and “ The Forgetting Machine.”
title: 《追忆风云》等超凡记忆案例(2019)
title_en: “Funes the Memorious” and Other Cases of Extraordinary Memory (2019)
